title: Putting up a shelf on a solid wall
subtitle: Find the fixings, drill, plug, level, and load
excerpt: How to fix a shelf bracket to a solid masonry wall: locating the correct wall type, drilling the right size hole for the wall plug, fitting the bracket, setting the shelf level, and loading it safely without exceeding the fixing capacity.
sourceNotes: Standard UK shelf-fixing practice. Rawlplug and Fischer technical data sheets for wall plug and anchor capacity. HSE guidance on safe working loads for domestic shelving.

recipeTools.notes: For drilling into softer brick and blockwork; the SDS is needed for hard masonry.
For marking drill positions square to the wall.

aliases: how to put up a shelf
fixing a shelf to a wall

content.text: A shelf on a solid wall is one of the most common domestic carpentry jobs. The failure mode is almost always the fixing, not the shelf itself: the wrong plug for the wall type, a hole drilled too small, or the bracket screwed into the plaster rather than the masonry behind it. Get the fixing right and the shelf is permanent.
Know your wall

UK solid walls are brick, dense block, aerated block (Thermalite or similar), or stone. Tap the wall: a dull solid sound indicates masonry; a hollow sound indicates either a cavity wall or a stud wall (see the stud-wall shelf article). The depth of plaster over masonry is typically 12 to 19 mm; the fixing must penetrate at least 50 mm into the masonry itself.
Marking out
Step 1, mark the shelf position.

Mark the desired shelf height at one end of the shelf run. Hold the spirit level along the wall at this height and draw a level pencil line for the full shelf length. Mark the bracket positions along this line: bracket centres no more than 600 mm apart for a loaded shelf, and no more than 100 mm from each end of the shelf board.
Drilling and plugging
Step 2, drill the fixing holes.

Hold the first bracket against the wall, align its top fixing hole with the level line, and mark the hole position on the wall. Repeat for all brackets. Drill with the SDS drill set to hammer-drill mode using a masonry bit matched to the wall plug size (typically 6 mm bit for a No. 10 / 6 mm plug). Drill to at least 60 mm depth.
Step 3, insert the wall plugs.

Blow the drill dust from the hole. Push the wall plug in by hand until it is flush with the plaster face. If the plug goes in loose, the hole is too large; use an oversize plug or a frame plug rated for the hole size.
Fitting the brackets
Step 4, fix the first bracket.

Hold the bracket against the wall with its top fixing hole over the plug. Drive the screw finger-tight first to confirm the bracket is aligned with the level line, then tighten fully. Fix all screws on the first bracket.
Step 5, fix the second bracket.
Hold the second bracket in position and rest the spirit level across both brackets. Adjust the height of the second bracket until the level reads plumb. Mark the fixing holes, drill, plug, and fix.
Fitting the shelf
Step 6, lay the shelf board and fix to the brackets.

Lay the shelf on the brackets. It should overhang equally at each end. Fix the shelf to the brackets with the supplied screws, driving through the bracket's top fixing lip into the underside of the shelf. Check level once more before final tightening.
Loading safely

A standard 6 mm wall plug and No. 10 × 50 mm screw into dense brick has a safe working load of about 30 kg per fixing. At 600 mm bracket spacing with two fixings per bracket, a run of four brackets can safely carry 120 kg total. Do not overload shelves; the point of failure is the fixing, and it fails suddenly.
Common mistakes
Screw will not tighten or pulls out.

Either the hole was drilled too large, the screw did not reach the masonry, or the plug was driven into aerated block, which has poor shear strength. Use a specialist aerated-block anchor or a chemical anchor for aerated block.
Shelf not level despite brackets aligned.
The shelf board is bowed. Check the shelf with a straightedge; pack any low spots between the shelf and the bracket top with a shim.
